payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
A track today rarely originates from a single act of authorship in a single DAW. It assembles. A producer pulls a drum loop from a sample marketplace, generates a stem from a music gen AI tool, drops in a vocal sent over by a collaborator, layers in a synth line they played themselves, and renders the master out the back of the DAW.

The DAW signs the assembly. That part is necessary. It is not sufficient.

If the upstream sources that contributed to the file are not signed at their own layer, the DAW's signature is binding only on the act of putting things together. It says nothing about what the things are or where they came from. That is the part of the chain where the most consequential questions live.

Three upstream surfaces

Modern music has at least three categories of upstream creation that need to sign at their own layer.

Sample and loop marketplaces are the first. A producer downloads a kick pattern, a melodic loop, a vocal hook. Each of those was created by someone at some point. The natural moment for that contribution to be signed is at upload to the marketplace. The marketplace already verifies producers at signup, manages payouts, and holds licensing terms. Signing the file at upload is small additional work on top of relationships that already exist.

Music gen AI tools are the second. Suno, Udio, ElevenLabs Music, and whatever ships next. The natural moment for those outputs to be signed is at generation, with the model identifier and the input prompt or seed bound into the manifest. This is operationally trivial. The tool already knows what model produced the output and what prompt drove it. The signature binds that information to the file in a way that survives downstream.

Collaborator stems are the third. Vocal takes, instrument stems, edits sent across by a co-writer or a session player. The natural moment to sign is at handoff. Every modern handoff path (Splice, Dropbox, the embedded sharing built into recent DAWs) could make this trivial. Today the file leaves the collaborator's hands carrying nothing structural about who made it.

The DAW pulls these signed inputs in. Its signature at export references them in the manifest. The output is not "signed by the DAW" alone. It is "signed by the DAW, built from these signed components, here are their credentials." A reader downstream can verify the chain end to end, or pick any link to inspect.

What breaks if any layer is missing

UPSTREAM CHAIN

SURFACE
SIGNS AT
STATUS

Sample marketplace
upload
✓ signed

Music gen AI
generation
✓ signed

Collaborator stem
handoff
✓ signed

Unsigned source
—
✗ chain broken

DAW
export
✓ signed

The chain is only as complete as its weakest link.

The chain is only as complete as its weakest link.

The temptation is to assume that signing the DAW output is enough. It is not.

A sample taken from an unsigned marketplace and assembled into a track produces a DAW signature that says "I signed this assembly." Downstream, a rights query asking who created the underlying loop has no answer. The signature does not deny it. It just does not speak to it. The chain continues with a gap, and a gap is what disputes drive through.

A gen-AI stem dropped in without its model and input manifest signed at the source produces the same problem. The DAW knows the stem was placed in the session. It does not know what the stem is. A label downstream looking at AI exposure across a release portfolio cannot answer the question "what percentage of this release used generative material" by reading the signed manifest. They can only read the assembly.

Multiply this across a hundred thousand new tracks released daily and the unsigned-input problem is the same kind of metadata mess the name tag system produces today, just relocated upstream.

Different verbs, same primitive

Each surface signs differently because each surface does different work.

The DAW signs at export because that is when the assembly is finalized.

The marketplace signs at upload because that is when the contribution enters the supply.

The gen AI tool signs at generation because that is when the output exists.

The collaborator signs at handoff because that is when the file leaves their custody.

Different verbs, different moments, same cryptographic primitive. The system does not care which surface a signature came from. It only cares that every link in the chain checks out.

The chain is the asset

The system's value is not in any single signature. It is in the chain.

A signed assembly with unsigned inputs is half the work, and the half that is missing is the half that is hardest to add later. Once the file is in distribution, identifying which loop came from where, which stem was generated, and which take was sent by whom is forensic work. With signatures at the source, it is a query.

The cryptographic primitives can do this. They have been doing it in other industries for decades. The question is not whether the upstream surfaces have the technology. It is whether they ship before the assembly layer demands it, and whether they coordinate on a shared format the DAW can consume.

The DAW's signature is the easy part. The inputs are where the work is.
payload.title: Signing the inputs at the source
